approaches.name: Brute Force O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all numbers in the range and check divisibility by each target number individually. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large ranges.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * k) where n is range size and k is number of divisors

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Divisible Numbers in Range
ladders.desc: Given a range [a,b] and a list of divisors, count how many numbers in the range are divisible by at least one divisor.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete problem that requires implementing the brute force O(n) approach. The intuition is straightforward: we need to check every number in the range [a,b] and see if it's divisible by any of the given divisors. If yes, we count it. This works because we're exhaustively examining every candidate number. The algorithm: 1) Initialize count = 0. 2) For each number num from a to b inclusive: 3) For each divisor d in divisors: 4) If num % d == 0: increment count and break (to avoid double-counting). 5) Return count. Time complexity is O((b-a+1)*k) where k is number of divisors, space is O(1). This connects to L1 by using the divisibility check as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in the range (forgetting to include b), not breaking after finding a divisor (wastes time but still correct), and integer overflow for large ranges. The brute force approach is conceptually simple but becomes impractical for very large ranges (e.g., a=1, b=10^9).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check Divisibility for a Single Number
ladders.desc: Given a number and a list of divisors, determine if it's divisible by any divisor.
ladders.explanation: This is the core checking function used in the full problem. The intuition: a number is divisible by a divisor if the remainder is zero when divided. We need to check all divisors until we find one that works. This works because divisibility is a binary property for each divisor. Algorithm: 1) For each divisor d in divisors: 2) If num % d == 0, return True. 3) If loop completes without finding, return False. Time: O(k) where k is divisors count, space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called for each number in the range. Common mistakes: returning True too early (should check all divisors unless instructed otherwise, but here we can break early for efficiency), incorrect modulo operation (e.g., using / instead of %), and not handling edge cases like divisor=0 (but problem guarantees positive divisors). The real pattern: def is_divisible(num, divisors): for d in divisors: if num % d == 0: return True; return False.

examples.trace: Check divisors: 12%3=0 → remainder 0, so divisible by 3. Return True immediately.
Check: 11%2=1, 11%3=2, 11%5=1. All remainders non-zero. Return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating Through a Range of Numbers
ladders.desc: Process every integer in a given inclusive range [a,b].
ladders.explanation: This is the main loop structure of the brute force approach. The intuition: to examine all numbers in a range, we need to visit each one sequentially. This works because the problem requires checking each number independently. Algorithm: 1) Initialize a counter or accumulator. 2) For num from a to b inclusive: 3) Perform some operation on num (e.g., check divisibility). 4) Update result based on operation. Time: O(n) where n=b-a+1, space: O(1). This connects to L1 by providing the numbers to check. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (using < instead of <= for inclusive range), not handling a>b case (but problem guarantees a<=b), and using while loops incorrectly. The pattern: for num in range(a, b+1): # do something with num. This is fundamental in many brute force solutions where we need to consider all possibilities in a discrete range.

examples.trace: Start num=3, process it. Increment num to 4, process. Continue to 5,6,7. After 7, loop ends.
num=10 → process. num=11 → process. num=12 → process. Done.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Operation for Divisibility Check
ladders.desc: Given two integers, check if one is divisible by the other using the modulo operator.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental operation to test divisibility. The intuition: a number x is divisible by d if x divided by d leaves remainder 0. The modulo operator % computes this remainder. This works because of the definition of divisibility in integer arithmetic. Algorithm: if x % d == 0, then divisible, else not. Time: O(1), space: O(1). This feeds into L2 as the condition checked for each number. Common mistakes: confusing modulo with division (e.g., using x/d == 0), not handling negative numbers (but problem uses non-negative), and integer division issues. The pattern: divisible = (x % d == 0). This is a basic building block in many number theory problems.

examples.trace: 10 % 2 = 0, so divisible.
7 % 3 = 1, remainder non-zero, so not divisible.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Looping Through a List
ladders.desc: Iterate over each element in a given list.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental programming operation used to process collections. The intuition: to examine each item in a list, we use a loop that visits them one by one. This works because lists are ordered sequences. Algorithm: for item in list: perform operation on item. Time: O(m) where m is list length, space: O(1). This connects to L3 by applying the modulo check to each divisor in the list. Common mistakes: modifying the list while iterating, using wrong loop bounds, and off-by-one errors in index-based loops. The pattern: for divisor in divisors: # check something with divisor. This is essential for handling multiple divisors in the problem.

examples.trace: First iteration: item=2, process it. Second: item=3, process. Third: item=5, process. Loop ends.
Only one item: item=10, process it. Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Variables and Basic Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Understand how to store and manipulate integer values.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic prerequisite: working with integer variables. The intuition: numbers can be stored in variables, and we can perform operations like addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, and modulo. This works because programming languages provide these fundamental data types and operations. Concepts: declaring variables (e.g., count = 0), updating them (count = count + 1), and using arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /, %). Time and space are O(1) per operation. This feeds into L4 as we use variables to store divisors, numbers, and counts. Common mistakes: using wrong operator symbols, not initializing variables, and integer division confusion in some languages. The pattern: a = 5; b = 3; sum = a + b; remainder = a % b. This foundation is necessary for any numerical computation in programming.

examples.trace: Start: count = 0. Execute: count = count + 1. Compute: 0 + 1 = 1. Store: count = 1.
10 divided by 3 is 3 with remainder 1. So 10 % 3 = 1.

approaches.name: Inclusion-Exclusion Principle O(2^k)
approaches.summary: Apply the inclusion-exclusion principle to count numbers divisible by any of the given divisors. Calculate counts for individual divisors and their intersections, then combine using the principle formula.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(2^k) where k is number of divisors

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Numbers Divisible by Any of Given Divisors Using Inclusion-Exclusion
ladders.desc: Given a range [L,R] and k divisors, count how many numbers in the range are divisible by at least one divisor using inclusion-exclusion principle.
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires counting numbers divisible by any of k given divisors within a range [L,R]. The inclusion-exclusion principle solves this by systematically accounting for overlaps between divisibility conditions. Intuitively, if we simply sum counts for each divisor, we overcount numbers divisible by multiple divisors. Inclusion-exclusion corrects this by alternately adding and subtracting counts for intersections of divisors. For k divisors, we consider all 2^k-1 non-empty subsets of divisors. For each subset, compute the least common multiple (LCM) of its divisors, count numbers divisible by this LCM in [L,R], then add or subtract based on subset size parity (odd: add, even: subtract). The algorithm: 1) Generate all non-empty subsets of divisors using bitmasking from 1 to 2^k-1. 2) For each subset, compute LCM of selected divisors. 3) Count numbers divisible by this LCM in [L,R] using formula: floor(R/LCM) - floor((L-1)/LCM). 4) Add this count if subset size is odd, subtract if even. 5) Return total. Time complexity is O(2^k * k) for subset generation and LCM computation, but typically simplified to O(2^k) as k is small. Space is O(1). This connects to L1 by using the subset LCM counting as the key subroutine. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle empty subset, incorrect LCM computation leading to overflow, off-by-one errors in range counting. Real code pattern: total=0; for mask in 1 to (1<<k)-1: lcm_val=1; bits=0; for i in 0 to k-1: if mask & (1<<i): lcm_val=lcm(lcm_val, divisors[i]); bits+=1; count = R//lcm_val - (L-1)//lcm_val; if bits%2==1: total+=count else: total-=count; return total.

examples.trace: Divisors: [2,3], k=2. Subsets: 1) mask=01 (divisor 2): LCM=2, count=20//2 - 0//2=10-0=10, bits=1 odd → add 10, total=10. 2) mask=10 (divisor 3): LCM=3, count=20//3 - 0//3=6-0=6, bits=1 odd → add 6, total=16. 3) mask=11 (divisors 2,3): LCM=lcm(2,3)=6, count=20//6 - 0//6=3-0=3, bits=2 even → subtract 3, total=13. Result=13.

Divisors: [3,5,7], k=3. mask=001: +30 → total=30. mask=010: +19 → total=49. mask=100: +13 → total=62. mask=011: -6 → total=56. mask=101: -4 → total=52. mask=110: -2 → total=50. mask=111: +0 → total=50. Result=50.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Count Numbers Divisible by LCM of a Subset in a Range
ladders.desc: Given a range [L,R] and a value LCM, count how many numbers in the range are divisible by LCM.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core computation used repeatedly in the inclusion-exclusion principle. For each subset of divisors, we compute their LCM and need to count numbers in [L,R] divisible by this LCM. Intuition: The count of multiples of LCM within a range can be found using integer division. Specifically, the number of integers x such that L ≤ x ≤ R and LCM divides x is given by floor(R/LCM) - floor((L-1)/LCM). This works because floor(R/LCM) counts multiples of LCM up to R, and floor((L-1)/LCM) counts multiples up to L-1, so their difference gives multiples in [L,R]. Step-by-step: 1) Compute count_up_to_R = R // LCM. 2) Compute count_up_to_L_minus_1 = (L-1) // LCM. 3) Return count_up_to_R - count_up_to_L_minus_1. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called for each subset in the inclusion-exclusion loop. Common mistakes: not handling L=1 correctly (L-1=0), integer overflow if LCM is large, misunderstanding floor division for negative numbers (but L,R positive here). Real code pattern: def count_divisible(L, R, LCM): return R//LCM - (L-1)//LCM.

examples.trace: Compute count_up_to_R = 20//3 = 6 (multiples: 3,6,9,12,15,18). Compute count_up_to_L_minus_1 = (5-1)//3 = 4//3 = 1 (multiples up to 4: 3). Result = 6 - 1 = 5 (multiples in [5,20]: 6,9,12,15,18).
count_up_to_R = 100//7 = 14. count_up_to_L_minus_1 = (1-1)//7 = 0//7 = 0. Result = 14 - 0 = 14.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Inclusion-Exclusion Principle for Multiple Conditions
ladders.desc: Apply inclusion-exclusion principle to combine counts from multiple divisibility conditions.
ladders.explanation: The inclusion-exclusion principle is a combinatorial technique to count elements in the union of multiple sets. For divisibility conditions, each divisor defines a set of numbers divisible by it. To count numbers divisible by at least one divisor (union of sets), we sum counts for individual divisors, subtract counts for pairwise intersections, add back counts for triple intersections, and so on. Intuition: When we add counts for individual divisors, numbers divisible by multiple divisors are counted multiple times. Inclusion-exclusion corrects this by alternately adding and subtracting intersection counts. Formally, for sets A1, A2, ..., Ak, |A1 ∪ A2 ∪ ... ∪ Ak| = Σ|Ai| - Σ|Ai ∩ Aj| + Σ|Ai ∩ Aj ∩ Al| - ... + (-1)^(k+1)|A1 ∩ A2 ∩ ... ∩ Ak|. For divisibility, intersection of sets corresponds to numbers divisible by the LCM of the corresponding divisors. In practice, we iterate over all non-empty subsets of divisors, compute the count for the LCM of each subset, and add or subtract based on subset size parity (odd size: add, even size: subtract). This is the central insight that transforms the problem from exponential enumeration to efficient O(2^k) computation. Time O(2^k) for subset iteration, space O(1). This connects to L1 by using the count for each subset's LCM. Common mistakes: incorrect sign handling, missing subsets, not considering all intersections up to k-way. Real code pattern: total=0; for each non-empty subset: compute count for LCM; if subset size odd: total+=count else total-=count.

examples.trace: Apply inclusion-exclusion: Start total=0. Add count for divisor 2 (size 1 odd): +10, total=10. Add count for divisor 3 (size 1 odd): +6, total=16. Subtract count for pair [2,3] (size 2 even): -3, total=13. Result=13.

examples.input: divisors=[2,3,5], counts: single: 2→50, 3→33, 5→20; pairs: LCM 6→16, LCM 10→10, LCM 15→6; triple: LCM 30→3
examples.trace: Total=0. Add singles: +50=50, +33=83, +20=103. Subtract pairs: -16=87, -10=77, -6=71. Add triple: +3=74. Result=74.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Least Common Multiple (LCM) of Multiple Numbers
ladders.desc: Calculate the LCM of two or more integers, which represents the intersection of their divisibility sets.
ladders.explanation: The least common multiple (LCM) of numbers is the smallest positive integer divisible by all of them. In inclusion-exclusion for divisibility, the intersection of sets for divisors d1, d2, ..., dm corresponds to numbers divisible by LCM(d1, d2, ..., dm). Intuition: If a number is divisible by both d1 and d2, it must be divisible by any common multiple; the smallest such is the LCM, and all common multiples are multiples of the LCM. To compute LCM of multiple numbers, we use the property: LCM(a,b) = a*b / GCD(a,b), and extend iteratively: LCM(a,b,c) = LCM(LCM(a,b), c). Step-by-step: Start with lcm_val = 1. For each number in the list: lcm_val = lcm_val * num // gcd(lcm_val, num). Continue until all numbers processed. Time O(m * log(max(num))) for m numbers, due to GCD computations. Space O(1). This connects to L2 by providing the LCM for each subset in inclusion-exclusion. Common mistakes: integer overflow if product a*b is large (use division before multiplication: lcm = a // gcd(a,b) * b), incorrect handling of zero (LCM is undefined for zero, but divisors are positive here). Real code pattern: def lcm(a,b): return a // gcd(a,b) * b; for multiple numbers: result=1; for num in nums: result = lcm(result, num).

examples.trace: Compute LCM(6,10). First, gcd(6,10)=2. Then LCM = 6*10 / 2 = 60/2 = 30.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) Using Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Calculate the GCD of two integers, which is needed for LCM computation.
ladders.explanation: The greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers is the largest positive integer that divides both without remainder. It's fundamental for computing LCM because LCM(a,b) = a*b / GCD(a,b). The Euclidean algorithm efficiently computes GCD based on the property: GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b). Intuition: If d divides both a and b, then it also divides a - b, and by extension a mod b. By repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder, we reduce the problem until the remainder is zero, at which point the other number is the GCD. Step-by-step: 1) While b ≠ 0: set a, b = b, a % b. 2) Return a. This works because GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b). Time O(log(min(a,b))) as numbers reduce quickly. Space O(1). This connects to L3 as a subroutine for LCM calculation. Common mistakes: not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loop if not updating variables correctly. Real code pattern: def gcd(a,b): while b: a, b = b, a % b; return abs(a).

examples.trace: Start a=48, b=18. Iteration 1: b=18 ≠0, compute a,b = 18, 48%18=12. Now a=18, b=12. Iteration 2: b=12 ≠0, compute a,b = 12, 18%12=6. Now a=12, b=6. Iteration 3: b=6 ≠0, compute a,b = 6, 12%6=0. Now a=6, b=0. Loop ends, return a=6.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Counting Multiples in a Range Using Integer Division
ladders.desc: Given a range [L,R] and a divisor d, count how many numbers in the range are divisible by d.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: counting multiples of a number within an interval. Intuition: The multiples of d are evenly spaced by d, so we can use integer division to count them without iterating. Specifically, the number of integers x such that L ≤ x ≤ R and d divides x is floor(R/d) - floor((L-1)/d). Here, floor(R/d) counts multiples of d from 1 to R, and floor((L-1)/d) counts multiples from 1 to L-1; subtracting gives multiples in [L,R]. Step-by-step: 1) Compute count_to_R = R // d. 2) Compute count_to_L_minus_1 = (L-1) // d. 3) Result = count_to_R - count_to_L_minus_1. Time O(1), space O(1). This foundational concept feeds into L4 and above, as it's used in counting for each divisor and LCM. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors with L-1, not using integer floor division correctly. Real code pattern: def count_multiples(L,R,d): return R//d - (L-1)//d.

examples.trace: Compute count_to_R = 15//4 = 3 (multiples: 4,8,12). Compute count_to_L_minus_1 = (3-1)//4 = 2//4 = 0. Result = 3 - 0 = 3.
count_to_R = 10//3 = 3. count_to_L_minus_1 = (1-1)//3 = 0//3 = 0. Result = 3 - 0 = 3.

approaches.name: LCM-Optimized Inclusion-Exclusion O(2^k)
approaches.summary: Enhance the inclusion-exclusion approach by using least common multiples (LCMs) for intersection calculations. This handles cases where divisors aren't coprime more efficiently.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(2^k * log M) where M is max divisor value

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Numbers Divisible by Any of K Divisors Using LCM-Optimized Inclusion-Exclusion
ladders.desc: Given N and K divisors, count numbers ≤ N divisible by at least one divisor using inclusion-exclusion with LCM optimization.
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires counting numbers from 1 to N that are divisible by at least one of K given divisors. The naive approach of checking each number against all divisors is O(N*K), which is inefficient for large N. The inclusion-exclusion principle provides a combinatorial solution: count numbers divisible by each divisor, subtract overlaps (divisible by two divisors), add back triple overlaps, etc. However, when divisors aren't coprime, simple multiplication for intersections (like divisible by both a and b) gives incorrect counts because it counts numbers divisible by the least common multiple (LCM), not the product. For example, with divisors 2 and 4, numbers divisible by both are those divisible by LCM(2,4)=4, not 2*4=8. The LCM-optimized approach enhances inclusion-exclusion by using LCM for intersection calculations, ensuring correctness for non-coprime divisors. The intuition is that the set of numbers divisible by multiple divisors corresponds to those divisible by their LCM. The algorithm generates all 2^K subsets of divisors (excluding the empty set), computes the LCM for each subset, counts numbers divisible by that LCM as floor(N/LCM), and adds or subtracts based on subset size parity (add for odd size, subtract for even). This works because inclusion-exclusion systematically accounts for overlaps using the principle of overcounting and correction. Time complexity is O(2^K * log M) where M is the max divisor value, due to LCM computations (using gcd, which is O(log M)). Space is O(1) excluding input storage. Connection to L1: This uses the subset generation and LCM calculation from L1 as a key subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle large N (use integer division), incorrect sign application (odd vs. even subsets), and not using LCM for intersections (leading to wrong counts for non-coprime divisors). To avoid these, always compute LCM via LCM(a,b)=a*b/gcd(a,b) and verify with examples. Real code pattern: Initialize total=0; loop over mask from 1 to (1<<K)-1; compute lcm=1 and count=0 for current subset; for each bit set, update lcm = lcm * divisors[i] // gcd(lcm, divisors[i]); if lcm > N, break (no numbers divisible); count = N // lcm; if subset size odd, total += count else total -= count; return total.

examples.trace: Step 1: List subsets of divisors (size>0). Subset {2}: LCM=2, count=floor(20/2)=10, sign=+ (size odd). Subset {3}: LCM=3, count=floor(20/3)=6, sign=+. Subset {4}: LCM=4, count=floor(20/4)=5, sign=+. Subset {2,3}: LCM=LCM(2,3)=6, count=floor(20/6)=3, sign=- (size even). Subset {2,4}: LCM=LCM(2,4)=4, count=floor(20/4)=5, sign=-. Subset {3,4}: LCM=LCM(3,4)=12, count=floor(20/12)=1, sign=-. Subset {2,3,4}: LCM=LCM(2,3,4)=12, count=floor(20/12)=1, sign=+ (size odd). Step 2: Sum with signs: total = 10+6+5 -3-5-1 +1 = 13. Verification: Numbers divisible by any of 2,3,4 up to 20: 2,3,4,6,8,9,10,12,14,15,16,18,20 → 13 numbers.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate Subsets and Compute LCM for Inclusion-Exclusion
ladders.desc: Given a list of divisors, generate all non-empty subsets and compute their LCM.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the LCM-optimized inclusion-exclusion approach. It involves iterating over all 2^K subsets of the K divisors (excluding the empty set) and for each subset, computing the least common multiple (LCM) of the divisors in that subset. The intuition is that in inclusion-exclusion, we need to consider intersections of sets (e.g., numbers divisible by both divisor A and divisor B), which correspond to the LCM of A and B, not their product, especially when they are not coprime. By generating subsets, we systematically cover all possible intersections. The algorithm uses bitmasking to represent subsets: for mask from 1 to (1<<K)-1, each bit position i corresponds to including the i-th divisor if the bit is set. For each mask, initialize lcm=1, then iterate over bits: if bit i is set, update lcm = lcm * divisors[i] // gcd(lcm, divisors[i]). If at any point lcm exceeds N (the upper bound), we can break early since no numbers ≤ N will be divisible by that LCM. This optimization reduces unnecessary computations. Time complexity is O(2^K * log M) per subset due to gcd computations, and space is O(1) for variables. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called within the main loop of L0 to compute counts for each subset. Common mistakes include incorrect LCM computation (e.g., using product instead of LCM), not handling integer overflow (use integer division carefully), and missing the empty subset (which should be excluded as it doesn't correspond to any divisibility condition). To avoid, always use LCM formula and test with small cases. Real code pattern: for mask in range(1, 1<<K): lcm=1; for i in range(K): if mask & (1<<i): lcm = lcm * divisors[i] // math.gcd(lcm, divisors[i]); if lcm > N: break; # process lcm for count.

examples.trace: Step 1: K=3, masks 1 to 7. Mask 1 (001): subset {4}, lcm=4. Mask 2 (010): subset {3}, lcm=3. Mask 3 (011): subset {3,4}, lcm=LCM(3,4)=12. Mask 4 (100): subset {2}, lcm=2. Mask 5 (101): subset {2,4}, lcm=LCM(2,4)=4. Mask 6 (110): subset {2,3}, lcm=LCM(2,3)=6. Mask 7 (111): subset {2,3,4}, lcm=LCM(2,3,4)=LCM(6,4)=12. All LCMs ≤20, so no break.
Step 1: K=2, masks 1 to 3. Mask 1 (01): subset {10}, lcm=10. Mask 2 (10): subset {6}, lcm=6. Mask 3 (11): subset {6,10}, lcm=LCM(6,10)=30. All LCMs ≤50.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Inclusion-Exclusion Principle with LCM for Set Intersections
ladders.desc: Apply inclusion-exclusion to count union of sets, using LCM to compute intersection sizes.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the combinatorial logic of inclusion-exclusion adapted for divisibility counts. Given sets A_i = numbers divisible by divisor d_i, we want |A_1 ∪ A_2 ∪ ... ∪ A_K|. The inclusion-exclusion principle states: |∪ A_i| = Σ|A_i| - Σ|A_i ∩ A_j| + Σ|A_i ∩ A_j ∩ A_k| - ... + (-1)^(K+1)|∩ A_i|. For divisibility, |A_i| = floor(N/d_i). The key insight is that |A_i ∩ A_j| = numbers divisible by both d_i and d_j, which is floor(N / LCM(d_i, d_j)), not floor(N / (d_i * d_j)) unless d_i and d_j are coprime. Similarly, higher-order intersections use LCM of all divisors in the subset. This works because a number is divisible by multiple divisors if and only if it is divisible by their LCM, as LCM is the smallest positive integer divisible by all. The algorithm thus sums over subsets: for each non-empty subset S, compute lcm = LCM of divisors in S, count = floor(N / lcm), and add or subtract count based on |S| parity (add if odd, subtract if even). Intuition: we start by adding counts for individual sets (overcounting overlaps), subtract pairwise overlaps (correcting overcount but now undercounting triple overlaps), add back triple overlaps, etc. Time complexity for this logic is O(2^K) for subset iteration, with LCM computation adding O(log M). Space is O(1). Connection to L1: This logic uses the subset generation and LCM computation from L1. Common mistakes include incorrect sign (adding for even subsets), using product instead of LCM, and not handling cases where lcm > N (count should be 0). To avoid, derive with small examples and verify parity. Real code pattern: total=0; for each subset S with lcm computed: count = N // lcm; if len(S) % 2 == 1: total += count else: total -= count.

examples.input: N=20, sets: |A|=10 (div by 2), |B|=6 (div by 3), |C|=5 (div by 4), intersections: |A∩B|=3 (LCM 6), |A∩C|=5 (LCM 4), |B∩C|=1 (LCM 12), |A∩B∩C|=1 (LCM 12)
examples.trace: Step 1: Apply inclusion-exclusion: |A∪B∪C| = |A|+|B|+|C| - |A∩B| - |A∩C| - |B∩C| + |A∩B∩C| = 10+6+5 -3-5-1 +1 = 13.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Least Common Multiple (LCM) of Multiple Numbers
ladders.desc: Calculate the LCM of a list of integers, handling non-coprime cases.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential for correctly computing intersection sizes in inclusion-exclusion. The least common multiple (LCM) of two numbers a and b is the smallest positive integer divisible by both a and b. For multiple numbers, LCM can be computed iteratively: LCM(a,b,c) = LCM(LCM(a,b), c). The formula for two numbers is LCM(a,b) = a * b / GCD(a,b), where GCD is the greatest common divisor. This works because the product a*b counts common factors twice, so dividing by GCD removes the overlap. For example, LCM(4,6) = 4*6/GCD(4,6)=24/2=12. The intuition is that LCM represents the union of prime factors with maximum exponents. To compute LCM for a list, start with lcm=1, then for each number x in the list, update lcm = lcm * x // GCD(lcm, x). This ensures integer division and avoids overflow by dividing before multiplication if needed. Time complexity is O(n log M) for n numbers, where M is the max value, due to GCD computations. Space is O(1). Connection to L2: This LCM computation is used to find intersection sizes in the inclusion-exclusion logic. Common mistakes include using product without GCD (incorrect for non-coprime numbers), integer overflow (use // for division in Python), and not handling zero (LCM is undefined for zero, but divisors are positive here). To avoid, always use the formula and test with examples. Real code pattern: import math; lcm=1; for x in list: lcm = lcm * x // math.gcd(lcm, x).

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute GCD(4,6)=2. Step 2: LCM = 4*6//2 = 24//2 = 12.
Step 1: Start lcm=1. Step 2: For 2: lcm = 1*2//GCD(1,2)=2//1=2. Step 3: For 3: lcm = 2*3//GCD(2,3)=6//1=6. Step 4: For 4: lcm = 6*4//GCD(6,4)=24//2=12.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) Using Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Find the GCD of two integers efficiently using the Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers a and b is the largest positive integer that divides both a and b without remainder. It is a fundamental operation needed for LCM computation. The Euclidean algorithm is an efficient method based on the property that GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b). The intuition is that if d divides both a and b, then it also divides a - b, and by extension, a mod b (the remainder). By repeatedly replacing (a,b) with (b, a mod b), we reduce the numbers until b becomes 0, at which point a is the GCD. For example, GCD(48,18): 48 mod 18 = 12, so GCD(48,18)=GCD(18,12); 18 mod 12=6, so GCD(18,12)=GCD(12,6); 12 mod 6=0, so GCD(12,6)=6. The algorithm can be implemented iteratively or recursively. Time complexity is O(log min(a,b)) as the numbers decrease quickly. Space is O(1) for iterative version. Connection to L3: GCD is used in the LCM formula LCM(a,b)=a*b/GCD(a,b). Common mistakes include not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loops if not reducing properly, and integer overflow in other languages (but safe in Python). To avoid, ensure the base case b==0 and use while loop. Real code pattern: def gcd(a,b): while b: a, b = b, a % b; return abs(a).

examples.trace: Step 1: a=48, b=18. Step 2: Compute 48 % 18 = 12, set a=18, b=12. Step 3: Compute 18 % 12 = 6, set a=12, b=6. Step 4: Compute 12 % 6 = 0, set a=6, b=0. Step 5: b=0, return a=6.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Divisibility and Counting Multiples Up to N
ladders.desc: Count how many integers from 1 to N are divisible by a given divisor d.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: given a positive integer N and a divisor d, count the numbers in the range [1, N] that are divisible by d. Divisibility means that when the number is divided by d, the remainder is 0. The multiples of d up to N are d, 2d, 3d, ..., up to the largest multiple ≤ N. This forms an arithmetic sequence. The count is simply the floor division of N by d, i.e., N // d in integer division. For example, for N=10 and d=3, multiples are 3,6,9 → count = 3, which is floor(10/3)=3. The intuition is that every d-th number is divisible by d, so the number of such numbers is approximately N/d, and floor division gives the exact integer count. This works because we start counting from 1; if we included 0, it would be floor(N/d) + 1, but typically we consider positive integers. Time complexity is O(1), space O(1). Connection to L4: This counting is used as a basic step in inclusion-exclusion for individual sets. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., counting 0 or N+1), using floating division without floor, and not handling d > N (count should be 0). To avoid, always use integer division and test edge cases. Real code pattern: count = N // d if d > 0 else 0.

examples.trace: Step 1: Multiples of 2 up to 10: 2,4,6,8,10. Step 2: Count = 5. Step 3: Verify with floor division: 10 // 2 = 5.
Step 1: Multiples of 3 up to 7: 3,6. Step 2: Count = 2. Step 3: 7 // 3 = 2.
